criterion_text: Title/abstract explicitly indicates that the paper specifically covers the use of NLP for process extraction from natural language text.
Observable task signal links natural-language text to process-model or process-representation extraction/construction objectives.
When the core fit is plausible but title/abstract evidence is incomplete, keep maybe and defer final confirmation to Stage 2.
Title/abstract clearly indicates the study is not specifically covering NLP for process extraction from natural language text.
Observable non-target examples: NLP for process redesign, matching, or process prediction.
Observable non-target examples: sentiment analysis, label-only targets, or generating natural text from processes.
Survey/review papers are excluded when title/abstract clearly indicates secondary research.
Paper is a full research article published in a peer-reviewed conference or journal, a full text is available, and the paper is written in English.
Paper is a primary research article presenting an original research contribution.
Paper specifically covers the use of NLP for process extraction from natural language text.
Paper explicates a concrete method and describes experiments to empirically validate the method.
Exclude papers that are not full research papers, not in a conference or journal, not peer-reviewed including preprints, without full text, or not in English.
Exclude secondary research articles.
Exclude papers not specifically covering NLP for process extraction, including redesign, matching, prediction, sentiment, label-only, or text-from-process generation.
Exclude papers that only superficially discuss the method or do not describe experiments.
Title/abstract indicates preference-learning evidence in audio, such as ranking or A/B comparison of two or more audio clips.
Numeric ratings count as preference evidence only when converted to rankings or comparative preference signals.
Papers without explicit comparisons remain eligible when title/abstract indicates an RL training loop for an audio model.
Paper is applied within the audio domain; multimodal works including audio are within the audio domain.
Paper is not a survey or review article.
Exclude studies that use preferences solely for evaluation, with no learning component.
Exclude studies clearly not in the audio domain.
Exclude survey or review articles.

Paper pertains to preference learning: ranking/A-B comparison of audio clips, numeric ratings only when converted to rankings, human or synthetic preferences, or RL loop for an audio model without explicit comparisons.
Paper is applied within the audio domain; multimodal works including audio are within the audio domain.
Paper is not a survey or review article.
Exclude studies that use preferences solely for evaluation, with no learning component.
Exclude survey or review articles.
